title: The Sky This Week from July 10 to 17: The Moon and Venus share Leo
summary:
Friday, July 10With no Moon in the sky tonight, it’s a great opportunity to chase after fainter objects, such as the Cat’s Eye Nebula (NGC 6543) in Draco. High in the north a few hours after sunset, this planetary nebula is a great option for any telescope, since it glows at a bright magnitude 8.1. Continue reading "The Sky This Week from July 10 to 17: The Moon and Venus share Leo"

The post The Sky This Week from July 10 to 17: The Moon and Venus share Leo appeared first on Astronomy Magazine.

So you're all set! 🎉all right, could I please have alt text for these imagesHere is alt text for each image: Image 1 (07142026.png): Planetarium simulation of the eastern sky on July 14 at 4:30 AM. Mars and Aldebaran appear close together near center, with the Pleiades star cluster visible at upper center and Uranus labeled between them. Image 2 (07162026.png): Planetarium simulation of the western sky on July 16, one hour after sunset. Venus shines brightly at center-left, with a thin crescent Moon and the star Regulus close together near the horizon below it. Image 3 (07172026.png): Planetarium simulation of the western sky on July 17 at 9:40 PM. A thin crescent Moon and Venus appear side by side low on the horizon, with the Melotte 111 star cluster (the Coma Star Cluster) labeled near the top of the frame. Image 4 (M29-2.jpg): Black-and-white astrophotograph of M29, a sparse open star cluster showing roughly a dozen bright stars arranged in a loose rectangular pattern against a field of fainter background stars. Image 5 (ASY-ST0723_01.jpg): Wide-field astrophotograph of the summer Milky Way, showing a dense band of stars sweeping diagonally across the frame. Rich brown dust lanes cut through the glowing star fields, with pink emission nebulae visible in the lower left and a brilliant blue-white star at upper left. Image 6 (saturn-moons-07122026.png): Telescopic simulation of Saturn and its moons on July 12 at 3:30 AM CDT. Saturn with its rings is centered, with moons Tethys and Rhea to the left, Dione to the right, and Titan below. A 30-arcsecond scale bar appears at the bottom center.Could you please add the year, 2026, to the text that mentions dates?Of course! fullArticle:
Friday, July 10With no Moon in the sky tonight, it’s a great opportunity to chase after fainter objects, such as the Cat’s Eye Nebula (NGC 6543) in Draco. High in the north a few hours after sunset, this planetary nebula is a great option for any telescope, since it glows at a bright magnitude 8.1.

You can find the Cat’s Eye just over 5° east of 3rd-magnitude Zeta (ζ) Draconis. Through a telescope, you’ll see the bright inner region of the nebula is quite compact, just 20” across. It’s surrounded by a larger halo nearly 6’ in extent. In physical space, it extends almost half a light-year. To most observers, the Cat’s Eye appears greenish in color, and its outer halo may look more oblong than spherical.

This is a young nebula — astronomers estimate it is only some 1,000 years old. Take so...
